text:
4 clocks per instruction = 4 million instructions per second (for common instructions, excluding multiplies, memory, etc.)?

This is amazing news.

elements.text:

text: It is _eye-opening_ how short all the files in <https://github.com/grokthis/ucisc/tree/master/hardware/ecp5_single_IPC> are.

eye-opening

text: The ecp5 version is an old implementation that never worked on hardware. The reference folder is the one to look at. In the end, the bulk of it is just one file cpu.v which is much smaller than I would have thought given what it does.

elements.text: The ecp5 version is an old implementation that never worked on hardware. The reference folder is the one to look at. In the end, the bulk of it is just one file cpu.v which is much smaller than I would have thought given what it does.

text: Yes, 4 million instructions per second for all instructions except division and TBD on multiplication. That's any combination of to/from memory including memory to memory with the other ALU operations in between.

elements.text: Yes, 4 million instructions per second for all instructions except division and TBD on multiplication. That's any combination of to/from memory including memory to memory with the other ALU operations in between.

text: the whole point of the paper and the talk is about defining expressiveness in a mathematical rigorous and unambiguous way, so while you think Felleisen redefined an existing word, he probably thinks the definition he found is way more precise and useful

elements.text: the whole point of the paper and the talk is about defining expressiveness in a mathematical rigorous and unambiguous way, so while you think Felleisen redefined an existing word, he probably thinks the definition he found is way more precise and useful

text: in any case I don't think you need to worry too much about your lawn because I suspect most people don't use Felleisen's rigorous meaning of expressiveness when they talk about languages :slightly_smiling_face:

elements.text: in any case I don't think you need to worry too much about your lawn because I suspect most people don't use Felleisen's rigorous meaning of expressiveness when they talk about languages

text:
I was kidding about the lawn, hopefully that was obvious :slightly_smiling_face: I don't really have a lawn if I go toe to toe against Shriram Krishnamurthy. This thread was an effort to spread the word on what they did. Hopefully by highlighting what got in the way for me so others who share the misconception don't spend as much time not getting it.

Particularly when doing a retrospective, it feels like a missed opportunity to not point out the two possible meanings. (I disagree that Shriram means what I mean, just in a more precise way. The two interpretations here have little in common. Sometimes we need to contrast two Lisp macros and debate which is more expressive, more clearly conveying intention. This paper wouldn't help there.)

elements.text: I was kidding about the lawn, hopefully that was obvious

Particularly when doing a retrospective, it feels like a missed opportunity to not point out the two possible meanings. (I disagree that Shriram means what I mean, just in a more precise way. The two interpretations here have little in common. Sometimes we need to contrast two Lisp macros and debate which is more expressive, more clearly conveying intention. This paper wouldn't help there.)

text: 55:30 (<https://youtu.be/43XaZEn2aLc?t=3329>)

My early goals, at least currently, are 1) VGA 640x480 screen, 2) UART for text input, 3) nintendo era game controllers - with that I can load code over UART, send text and "type" over UART, and program some simple "games". It's a really good POC that roughly equates to the capabilities of a C64 at that point and I can switch focus to better uCISC and higher level languages and see where I can take it.

elements.text: My early goals, at least currently, are 1) VGA 640x480 screen, 2) UART for text input, 3) nintendo era game controllers - with that I can load code over UART, send text and "type" over UART, and program some simple "games". It's a really good POC that roughly equates to the capabilities of a C64 at that point and I can switch focus to better uCISC and higher level languages and see where I can take it.

text: I took it as a tool for language designers, to be used something like: "go crazy with your features as long as they don't increase expressive power (while loops! for loops! do/while loops!), but as soon as your feature increases expressive power, be very careful in adding new stuff"

elements.text: I took it as a tool for language designers, to be used something like: "go crazy with your features as long as they don't increase expressive power (while loops! for loops! do/while loops!), but as soon as your feature increases expressive power, be very careful in adding new stuff"

text: also as an end user I always want to use the most powerful tool available, but according to this definition I could be making my life easier by finding a language with less expressive power that still is able to accomplish the given task

elements.text: also as an end user I always want to use the most powerful tool available, but according to this definition I could be making my life easier by finding a language with less expressive power that still is able to accomplish the given task

text:
Particularly if you have existing expectations.

If you're starting from scratch with a new system, you want it to be as powerful as possible. But _once you start using it_, moving the goalposts often feels hostile.

text: I'd propose to use the two terms "expressive" and "powerful"

elements.text: I'd propose to use the two terms "expressive" and "powerful"

text:
Expressive is the code giving you more information, more to reason about. E.g. for is more expressive than while.

However, while is more powerful than if, as it empowers the user: you can do more with it.

elements.text:

text: It's the other way around? `while` and `for` are of equal power because you can locally rewrite one into the other. The paper is really discussing power. "Local rewrites" is a finer gradation of power than the leap from context-sensitive to Turing-complete languages. But it feels like it belongs on that spectrum.

elements.text: It's the other way around?
while
and
for

are of equal power because you can locally rewrite one into the other. The paper is really discussing power. "Local rewrites" is a finer gradation of power than the leap from context-sensitive to Turing-complete languages. But it feels like it belongs on that spectrum.

text: The example of continuations (or shared mutable state threads heaven forbid) comes to mind: without them less weird stuff could happen at any one spot — in particular you can ignore some long distance interactions.  Coming back to macros, there's a difference between one that tidies up some local pattern and one that have a more spread-out effect.

elements.text: The example of continuations (or shared mutable state threads heaven forbid) comes to mind: without them less weird stuff could happen at any one spot — in particular you can ignore some long distance interactions.  Coming back to macros, there's a difference between one that tidies up some local pattern and one that have a more spread-out effect.

Interesting stuff. I am partial to the entity-attribube-value triples so I think this is a good start.

> Being honest to myself, I have to give up some of my language ideas and implement a simpler language.
+1. I don't think the substrate language should have advanced concepts. It should be something really simple and other features could be added in higher layers

text:
> It's the other way around? `while` and `for` are of equal power because you can locally rewrite one into the other.
Well. Yeah. I agree that they're the same power. However, I wouldn't say they're the other way around. In terms of power, I was comparing `while` and `if` before. In terms of expressiveness I was comparing `while` and `for`. Can you re-read my message to see whether we actually disagree or whether this was a misunderstanding?

text: I like an idea of treating code as data. Stared your project on github.

elements.text: I like an idea of treating code as data. Stared your project on github.

text: I like the idea of EAV data as a building block for programming languages!

elements.text: I like the idea of EAV data as a building block for programming languages!

text:
You might have noticed that my model is IEAV. Each EAV tuple has a unique id, so tuples are addressable, too. You can assign attributes and do anything you do with normal entities.

I use that to establish ordering of items, and I have a couple of other ideas as well. For example, you can represent diffs in IEAV by assigning added/removed attribute to tuples.

elements.text:
You might have noticed that my model is IEAV. Each EAV tuple has a unique id, so tuples are addressable, too. You can assign attributes and do anything you do with normal entities.

I use that to establish ordering of items, and I have a couple of other ideas as well. For example, you can represent diffs in IEAV by assigning added/removed attribute to tuples.

text: Yes I have noticed the unique id and I also really like the idea because this property makes your way of representing things a natural fit for CRDT like systems. I would like to use CRDT for offline first collaborative editing environments and I guess your approach will be perfect as a representation for editable artifacts that are managed by the environment, be it data or logic.

text:
After watching that talk on Datalog for 13 minutes, it wasn't hard to believe that datalog could be a better database than RDBMSs. But I'm biased - I've thought RDBMSs sucked for maybe 15 years or so. It sounds like this kind of DB will automatically optimize itself by automatically noticing when it would be useful to have a particular kind of index... or something?

Datalog has 5-tuples (entity, attribute, value, transaction_id, delete_flag). What is the id in [id, entity, attribute, value] and why is this used instead of the last two items in that tuple?

You said that "a list of id-entity-attribute-value tuples is enough to model any data." No doubt you could; it doesn't follow that you should though. In his talk Norbert Wojtowicz mentioned that there were cases when he wouldn't use Datalog for something.

When I looked at your code:
```identifier(0) : Attribute {
  Attribute.value-type = String
  comment = "..."
}```
I thought to myself, as I usually do, that this was similar to <http://loyc.net/les/|LES>. Have you heard of it? I'm just starting to ask people this question: (1) have your heard of LES or Loyc trees and (2) if you had heard about them, would you have considered using them (e.g. to make prototyping easier, or to separate the syntax of your language from its semantics)? Why or why not?
